data.content:
### Manual Trigger and Describe Workflow (Temporal CLI)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/temporal/llm_analytics/trace_clustering/README.md

Commands to manually start a `daily-trace-clustering` workflow using the Temporal CLI for development or testing. Includes a command to check the status of a running or completed workflow by its ID.

```bash
# Using Temporal CLI
temporal workflow start \
  --task-queue development-task-queue \
  --type daily-trace-clustering \
  --workflow-id "trace-clustering-test-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)" \
  --input '{"team_id": 1, "lookback_days": 7, "max_samples": 1000, "min_k": 2, "max_k": 10}'

# Check status
temporal workflow describe --workflow-id "trace-clustering-test-YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS"
```

--------------------------------

### Display All hogli Commands

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This command lists all available subcommands within the hogli CLI. Each subcommand is self-documenting and can be further explored using the --help flag.

```bash
hogli --help
```

--------------------------------

### Install and Use hogli via Flox

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This snippet shows the recommended way to activate the hogli CLI using Flox, making it available in your PATH for direct use. It also demonstrates a common initial command to get started.

```bash
flox activate
hogli quickstart
```

--------------------------------

### Run Toolbox CLI (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

These commands demonstrate how to execute the `toolbox.py` script. The first uses the Python interpreter, while the second assumes the script has been made executable.

```bash
python toolbox.py [flags]
```

```bash
./toolbox.py [flags]
```

--------------------------------

### Start Per-Team Trace Summarization Workflow (Temporal CLI)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/temporal/llm_analytics/trace_summarization/README.md

Initiates the per-team trace summarization workflow using the Temporal CLI. This command allows specifying the workflow type, task queue, workflow ID, and input parameters for the team ID and processing window.

```bash
temporal workflow start \
  --type llma-trace-summarization \
  --task-queue development-task-queue \
  --workflow-id "batch-summarization-team-1-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)" \
  --input '{"team_id": 1}'
```

```bash
# With custom parameters
temporal workflow start \
  --type llma-trace-summarization \
  --task-queue development-task-queue \
  --workflow-id "batch-summarization-team-1-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)" \
  --input '{"team_id": 1, "max_traces": 50, "window_minutes": 30}'
```

--------------------------------

### Get Help for a Specific hogli Command

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

Append '--help' to any hogli command to access detailed documentation and available options for that specific subcommand. This provides granular insight into command usage.

```bash
hogli test:python --help
```

--------------------------------

### Make Script Executable (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

This command makes the `toolbox.py` script executable on Unix-based systems. It is a prerequisite for running the script directly from the command line.

```bash
chmod +x toolbox.py
```

--------------------------------

### Orchestrating Commands with 'steps' in manifest.yaml

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This example shows how to define a command in `manifest.yaml` that orchestrates multiple existing hogli commands in sequence using the `steps` directive. This is suitable when no complex shell logic is required.

```yaml
dev:reset:
  steps:
    - docker:services:down
    - docker:services:up
    - migrations:run
    - dev:demo-data
  description: Full reset and reload
```

--------------------------------

### Build and Install Local PostHog CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/cli/CONTRIBUTING.md

This bash snippet shows how to build a new version of the posthog-cli locally and optionally replace an existing installation. It involves using `cargo install` to build the CLI from the local path and then copying the generated executable to override the system's installed version. This is useful for testing local changes.

```bash
cp ./target/release/posthog-cli "$(which posthog-cli)"
```

--------------------------------

### Install sqlx-cli and Run Migrations

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/manual-dev-setup.md

Installs the SQLx CLI tool for database migrations and then executes the migration script. This sets up the necessary database schema.

```bash
cargo install sqlx-cli # If you haven't already
DEBUG=1 ./bin/migrate
```

--------------------------------

### Delegating to Shell Scripts with 'bin_script' in manifest.yaml

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This snippet illustrates how to define a command in `manifest.yaml` that delegates execution to an external shell script. This is used when complex shell scripting, including loops and conditionals, is necessary. The script is placed in the `bin/` directory.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -e
# Shell logic: loops, conditionals, etc.
for i in {1..5}; do
    echo "Attempt $i..."
    if some_check; then break; fi
done
```

```yaml
check:my-service:
  bin_script: my-script
  description: Check if service is ready
  services: [docker]
```

--------------------------------

### Simple Shell Command with 'cmd' in manifest.yaml

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This example demonstrates defining a simple shell command directly within `manifest.yaml` using the `cmd` directive. This is appropriate for single-line shell commands without complex logic, such as invoking linters or build tools.

```yaml
lint:
  cmd: "./bin/ruff.sh check . && pnpm --filter=@posthog/frontend run lint"
  description: Run code quality checks
```

--------------------------------

### Start Full Development Stack with hogli

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This command initiates the complete PostHog development environment, including the backend, plugin server, workers, and frontend. It utilizes the underlying 'bin/start' script orchestrated by 'mprocs'.

```bash
hogli start
```

--------------------------------

### Python Click Command for Meta/Framework Operations

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This snippet shows how to add a Python Click command for meta-level operations that interact with hogli's internals. These commands are typically added to `common/hogli/core/cli.py` and are less common.

```python
# In common/hogli/core/cli.py
@cli.command(name="meta:my-command", help="Does something with hogli internals")
def my_meta_command() -> None:
    """Command implementation."""
    from hogli.core.manifest import load_manifest
    manifest = load_manifest()
    # Your logic here
    click.echo("Done!")
```

--------------------------------

### Run Fast Quality Checks with hogli

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This command executes quick quality checks such as linting for Python and JavaScript. It is designed to be fast (~5 minutes) and intentionally excludes slower tests.

```bash
hogli lint
```

--------------------------------

### AWS CLI Configuration for S3 Lifecycle Rules

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/internal/workflows/s3-query-cache-setup.md

Configures S3 lifecycle rules using the AWS CLI to automatically delete objects based on the 'ttl_days' tag. This script creates a JSON configuration file and applies it to a specified S3 bucket. You must ensure rules exist for all 'ttl_days' values generated by your application.

```bash
# Create rules for common TTL values: 1, 2, 7, 14, 30 days
cat > lifecycle-config.json << EOF
{
    "Rules": [
        {
            "ID": "query-cache-ttl-1-day",
            "Status": "Enabled",
            "Filter": {
                "And": {
                    "Tags": [
                        {"Key": "ttl_days", "Value": "1"},
                    ]
                }
            },
            "Expiration": {"Days": 1}
        },
        {
            "ID": "query-cache-ttl-7-days",
            "Status": "Enabled",
            "Filter": {
                "And": {
                    "Tags": [
                        {"Key": "ttl_days", "Value": "7"},
                    ]
                }
            },
            "Expiration": {"Days": 7}
        }
    ]
}
EOF

aws s3api put-bucket-lifecycle-configuration \
    --bucket your-bucket-name \
    --lifecycle-configuration file://lifecycle-config.json
```

--------------------------------

### Start Coordinator Trace Summarization Workflow (Temporal CLI)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/temporal/llm_analytics/trace_summarization/README.md

Starts the coordinator workflow for batch trace summarization using the Temporal CLI. This workflow manages spawning child workflows for multiple teams based on the allowlist.

```bash
temporal workflow start \
  --type llma-trace-summarization-coordinator \
  --task-queue development-task-queue \
  --workflow-id "batch-summarization-coordinator-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)" \
  --input '{}'
```

--------------------------------

### Python Click Command for Developer Workflows

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This snippet demonstrates how to add a custom Python Click command for developer workflows. It resides in `common/hogli/commands.py` and allows for complex Python logic, argument parsing, and multi-step Python operations.

```python
# In common/hogli/commands.py
from hogli.core.cli import cli
import click

@cli.command(name="my-workflow", help="Custom workflow command")
@click.option('--verbose', is_flag=True, help='Verbose output')
def my_workflow(verbose: bool) -> None:
    """Custom workflow implementation."""
    # Your Python logic here
    click.echo("Running custom workflow...")
```

--------------------------------

### Run Specific JavaScript Tests with hogli

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This command runs specific JavaScript tests within a given directory. It's advised to execute tests independently to avoid prolonged workflow interruptions.

```bash
hogli test:js frontend/src/scenes/Foo/
```

--------------------------------

### Run Specific Python Tests with hogli

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This command runs specific Python tests, allowing you to target individual test files for verification. It's recommended to run tests separately from other workflows due to their longer execution time.

```bash
hogli test:python posthog/api/test/test_foo.py
```

--------------------------------

### S3 Data Deletion by Prefix (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/rust/capture/docs/llma-capture-overview.md

This snippet demonstrates how to delete all S3 objects associated with a specific team using the AWS CLI. It targets objects under a given prefix, effectively removing all data for that team. This method is simple to implement but can be resource-intensive if a large number of objects need deletion.

```bash
aws s3 rm s3://posthog-llm-analytics/llma/123/ --recursive
```

--------------------------------

### Hiding Commands with 'hidden: true' in manifest.yaml

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This snippet shows how to hide a command from the `--help` output by setting `hidden: true` in `manifest.yaml`. This is useful for deprecated, production-only, or specialist commands that should still be callable but not clutter the help information.

```yaml
docker:deprecated:
  bin_script: docker
  description: '[DEPRECATED] Use `hogli start` instead'
  hidden: true # Still works, just not shown in --help
```

--------------------------------

### Connect to Toolbox Pod (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

Examples of running the `toolbox.py` script to connect to a PostHog toolbox pod. The first example uses the default claim duration, while the second specifies a custom duration.

```bash
python toolbox.py

```

```bash
python toolbox.py --claim-duration 4
```

--------------------------------

### Create Release Branch and Tag PostHog CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/cli/CONTRIBUTING.md

This snippet demonstrates the bash commands to create a new branch for a release, commit version bumps, and tag the release for the posthog-cli. It involves checking out a new branch, adding all changes, committing with a message, and pushing both the branch and tags. It also includes an optional step to publish to crates.io.

```bash
git checkout -b "cli/release-v0.1.0-pre1"
# 1. Bump version number in Cargo.toml
# 2. Build to update Cargo.lock (cargo build)
# 3. Update the CHANGELOG.md
git add .
git commit -m "Bump version number"
git tag "posthog-cli-v0.1.0-prerelease.1"
git push
git push --tags
# Optional - also publish to crates.io
cd cli && cargo publish
```

--------------------------------

### Define npm Workflow Commands in YAML

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This YAML structure defines a high-level workflow command for formatting code. It includes a 'cmd' to execute the npm script and a 'description'. A 'TODO' comment indicates potential future conversion to hogli steps.

```yaml
format:
  cmd: pnpm format
  description: Format backend and frontend code
  # TODO: candidate for conversion to hogli steps
```

--------------------------------

### Install and Use hogli without Flox

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This snippet illustrates how to use hogli without Flox. It involves synchronizing Python dependencies and then executing the hogli script directly from the bin directory. Ensure 'bin/' is in your PATH for direct invocation.

```bash
uv sync
bin/hogli quickstart
```

--------------------------------

### Extend Pod Claim Duration (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/infra-scripts/clitools/README.md

This command demonstrates how to use the `toolbox.py` script to extend the claim duration of an existing PostHog toolbox pod. It utilizes the `--update-claim` and `--claim-duration` flags.

```bash
python toolbox.py --update-claim --claim-duration 24
```

--------------------------------

### Associate Commands with Services in YAML

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This YAML snippet demonstrates how to declare service associations for a command. The 'services' array lists the services (e.g., docker, kafka, postgresql) that the command interacts with, enabling 'hogli meta:concepts' to display this relationship.

```yaml
command:
  bin_script: script
  services: [docker, kafka, postgresql]
```

--------------------------------

### Introspect pprof Profile Reports with go tool pprof

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/rust/common/profiler/README.md

Analyzes captured pprof profile reports using the `go tool pprof` command. Supports interactive web interfaces, CLI shells, and generating visual reports like PNGs and web-based flamegraphs. Including the service binary aids symbol translation for more detailed analysis.

```bash
# Unzip the report protobuf
$ unzip profile.pb.gz
# Use pprof tool to introspect on report. Basic syntax:
$ go tool pprof <options> <optional_path_to_local_debug_binary> <path_to_profile_report_file>

# Examples:

# Open interactive web interface to analyze report (recommended)
$ go tool pprof -http=localhost:<PORT> profile.pb

# Open interactive CLI shell with report
$ go tool pprof profile.pb

# Include service binary for additional symbol translation (YMMV, can add this arg to any of the commands listed here)
$ go tool pprof posthog/rust/target/debug/kafka-deduplicator profile.pb

# Open web browser with SVG image of profile report
$ go tool pprof -web profile.pb
# Generate and open a local PNG image of the profile report
$ go tool pprof -png profile.pb && open profile001.png
# Quick summary report
$ go tool pprof -top profile.pb
```

--------------------------------

### Enable Debug Logs for Backend Tests (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Adds the `--log-cli-level=DEBUG` argument to the pytest command to enable detailed debug logs, including ClickHouse queries. This is essential for diagnosing issues within the backend tests. The argument is appended to the pytest command.

```bash
pytest --log-cli-level=DEBUG
```

--------------------------------

### Run Specific Backend Tests by File Path (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Runs backend tests specified by a file path. This allows focusing on tests within a particular file, aiding in faster debugging and development cycles. It takes the file path as an argument.

```bash
pytest posthog/test/test_example.py
```

--------------------------------

### Run Backend Tests (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Executes all backend tests using pytest. This command ensures the backend logic and functionality are working correctly. No specific inputs are required for a full test run.

```bash
pytest
```

--------------------------------

### Self-Host PostHog Hobby Deploy (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/README.md

Deploys a PostHog hobby instance on Linux using Docker. Recommended for users with at least 4GB of memory. This script fetches and executes the deployment commands from a GitHub repository.

```bash
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/posthog/posthog/HEAD/bin/deploy-hobby)"
```

--------------------------------

### Run End-to-End Tests (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Executes end-to-end tests using the `bin/e2e-test-runner` script. This spins up a test instance of PostHog and launches the Cypress interface for manual test selection. Requires `uv` to be installed.

```bash
bin/e2e-test-runner
```

--------------------------------

### Run AI Endpoint Integration Tests

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/rust/capture/docs/llma-integration-test-suite.md

Commands to execute all AI endpoint integration tests, specific tests, or tests with detailed output. These tests are implemented using Tokio and axum-test-helper.

```bash
cargo test --test integration_ai_endpoint

cargo test --test integration_ai_endpoint test_multipart_parsing_with_multiple_blobs

cargo test --test integration_ai_endpoint -- --nocapture
```

--------------------------------

### PostHog Environment Variables for Vercel SSO

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/ee/api/vercel/README.md

This section details the necessary environment variables to be configured in a `.env.vercel` file for local Vercel SSO development. It includes settings for HTTPS mode, debug flags, ngrok origin URLs, and Vercel integration credentials, emphasizing the use of 1Password CLI for secret management.

```dotenv
# Enable HTTPS mode for local development
LOCAL_HTTPS=1
DEBUG=1

# ngrok tunnel URLs for HTTPS local development (update with your ngrok URL)
JS_URL=https://your-ngrok-tunnel.ngrok-free.dev
WEBPACK_HOT_RELOAD_HOST=0.0.0.0
NGROK_ORIGIN=https://your-ngrok-tunnel.ngrok-free.dev

# Vercel integration credentials (automatically loaded from 1Password)
VERCEL_CLIENT_INTEGRATION_ID="op://General/Vercel Client Integration Secret/client id"
VERCEL_CLIENT_INTEGRATION_SECRET="op://General/Vercel Client Integration Secret/client secret"
```

--------------------------------

### Create Django Migration (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Generates a new Django migration file. The `DEBUG=1` environment variable is used, which may influence migration behavior or logging. This command initiates the migration creation process.

```bash
DEBUG=1 ./manage.py makemigrations
```

--------------------------------

### Run Frontend Unit Tests (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Executes all frontend unit tests using pnpm. This is a standard command for ensuring the frontend codebase integrity. No specific inputs are required, and all tests are run.

```bash
pnpm test:unit
```

--------------------------------

### Run Specific Frontend Unit Tests by Path (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Allows running specific frontend unit tests by providing a file path pattern to Jest. This is useful for focusing on a particular test file or a set of related tests. It takes a test path pattern as input.

```bash
pnpm jest --testPathPattern=frontend/src/lib/components/IntervalFilter/intervalFilterLogic.test.ts
```

--------------------------------

### Install and Run NextJS App (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/frontend/src/scenes/surveys/CONTRIBUTING.md

Installs dependencies and runs the NextJS development server after updating the local `posthog-js` build. This command should be executed from the `playground/nextjs` directory.

```bash
cd playground/nextjs
rm -rf node_modules .next && pnpm install && pnpm dev
```

--------------------------------

### Run Specific Backend Tests by Function Name (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Executes backend tests that match a specific function name pattern. This is highly useful for isolating and running a single test case or a group of related tests. It requires the file path and a pattern for the function name.

```bash
pytest posthog/test/test_example.py -k test_something
```

--------------------------------

### Setup ANTLR Environment on Ubuntu (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/hogql/grammar/README.md

Sets up the ANTLR environment on Ubuntu using bash. This involves downloading a specific version of the ANTLR JAR, creating a script to run it, and configuring environment variables for CLASSPATH and PATH. This setup is necessary for using the ANTLR tool directly.

```bash
export ANTLR_VERSION=4.13.2

sudo apt-get install default-jre
mkdir antlr
cd antlr
curl -o antlr.jar https://www.antlr.org/download/antlr-$ANTLR_VERSION-complete.jar
export PWD=`pwd`
echo '#!/bin/bash' > antlr
echo "java -jar $PWD/antlr.jar $*" >> antlr
chmod +x antlr
export CLASSPATH=".:$PWD/antlr.jar:$CLASSPATH"
export PATH="$PWD:$PATH"
```

--------------------------------

### Run Trace Clustering Tests - Bash

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/temporal/llm_analytics/trace_clustering/README.md

Executes the test suite for the trace clustering workflow using pytest. This command helps ensure the integrity and correctness of the clustering logic, including K-means optimization and data validation. It targets a specific test file within the PostHog project.

```bash
pytest posthog/temporal/llm_analytics/trace_clustering/test_workflow.py -v
```

--------------------------------

### Update Specific Visual Regression Snapshots by Path (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Updates visual regression test snapshots only for stories under a specified path. This is useful for targeted updates when only a subset of visual tests needs refreshing. It requires the path to the story file as an argument.

```bash
pnpm test:visual:update frontend/src/lib/Example.stories.tsx
```

--------------------------------

### Environment Variables for GitHub App

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/products/tasks/backend/temporal/process_task/README.md

Configure the GitHub App integration by setting these environment variables in your .env file. The private key should be securely stored and can contain literal newline characters which will be interpreted correctly.

```bash
GITHUB_APP_CLIENT_ID=your_app_id
GITHUB_APP_SLUG=your-app-slug
GITHUB_APP_PRIVATE_KEY="-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
...\n-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----"
```

--------------------------------

### Verify Vercel Integration Credentials Loading

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/ee/api/vercel/README.md

This command snippet helps in verifying that the Vercel integration credentials are being correctly loaded from 1Password and the `.env.vercel` file. It prints environment variables related to Vercel, allowing developers to check for correct values or confirm that secrets are not being exposed.

```bash
# Authenticate with 1Password CLI first if needed
op whoami

# Test credential loading by printing VERCEL related environment variables
op run --env-file=.env.vercel -- printenv | grep VERCEL
```

--------------------------------

### Run Local Tests for hogql-parser on macOS

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogql_parser/CONTRIBUTING.md

Executes the test suite for the hogql module using pytest. This command verifies the functionality of the locally developed hogql_parser after installation.

```bash
pytest posthog/hogql/
```

--------------------------------

### Copy Environment File Example

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/services/mcp/examples/langchain/README.md

Copies the example environment file to a new file named `.env`. This file will store sensitive credentials such as API keys. After copying, you need to manually edit the `.env` file to include your specific keys.

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Local PostHog Test Environment Variables

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/rust/capture/docs/llma-acceptance-test-suite.md

Sets environment variables for the local PostHog test instance URL and personal API key. The POSTHOG_TEST_BASE_URL defaults to 'http://localhost:8010' if not specified, while POSTHOG_PERSONAL_API_KEY is mandatory.

```bash
# PostHog Instance (defaults to http://localhost:8010 if not set)
export POSTHOG_TEST_BASE_URL="http://localhost:8010"

# Personal API Key (required - no default)
export POSTHOG_PERSONAL_API_KEY="your_personal_api_key_here"
```

--------------------------------

### Query ClickHouse Local Data (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/products/llm_analytics/dags/daily_metrics/README.md

Executes a SQL query against the local ClickHouse instance to retrieve daily LLMA metrics. It requires Docker to be running and the 'posthog-clickhouse-1' container to be available. Outputs results in a human-readable format.

```bash
docker exec posthog-clickhouse-1 clickhouse-client --query "SELECT * FROM llma_metrics_daily WHERE date = today() FORMAT Pretty"
```
